items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.

items.q: What is the main social impact of gentrification?
items.correct: Displacement of long-term lower-income residents as rents and property prices rise, often along with loss of community businesses and cultural identity — even if the area looks 'improved'.
items.misconception: Gentrification only has positive effects because it makes areas nicer.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means evicting all residents by force.
items.why: Improvements aren't free — they're paid for by displacement. Students focus on visible upgrades and miss the demographic shift. Many evaluation questions ask 'for whom is gentrification beneficial?'.

items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.
Correct answer: Human Development Index. “High Development Indicator” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Norway. “UK” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many stages in Rostow's model?
items.why: The real figure is 5 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
